topic: Phase A1 design refresh integration spot-check (EXP-202, 207, 208, 209, 210, 212, 213)
change_summary: Phase A1 integrated: typography tokens + WOFF2 fonts (EXP-202), three-role font assignment (EXP-207), masthead stance-statement (EXP-208), verdict distribution bar chart (EXP-209), red-chrome neutralization to semantic-crit tokens (EXP-210), Latest Findings demotion (EXP-212), accessibility fixes — glyphs + labels, 44px tap targets, stacked-card mobile tables (EXP-213).
our_argument_summary: The Phase A1 design refresh reframes the Overview tab around three additions (stance-statement, verdict bar chart) and several neutralizations (red chrome, Latest Findings demotion, color+glyph accessibility). The structural intent — reduce dashboard feel, let prose carry the argument, keep severity signal honest — is consistent with the external designer brief and holds up in the rendered HTML.

dome_code_analysis.calculation_pipeline: Not applicable — design-refresh EXPs do not touch any monitor.py / pull_data.py automation. The changes are CSS tokens, template insertions, and one new generator function (generateVerdictBarChart).
dome_code_analysis.hardcoded_vs_computed: The verdict bar chart is computed live from data/wins.json via generateVerdictBarChart(tally, wins.length). Counts are correct (71 total, tally sums to 71). Sort-by-count with alphabetical tiebreak is deterministic.
data_sources_used: data/sections.json (three prose blobs touched for font-family migration)
dome_code_analysis.code_discrepancies: None material — generator logic matches EXP-209 spec; ANCHOR map for Unfalsifiable → #misleading is explicitly documented in the code comment and matches the combined section 3.6 heading.

code_analysis_tags.monitoring_evidence: This is a design-integration spot-check, not a WIN. The dome monitor.py code is irrelevant to CSS/template changes.

holes_found.description: Claim-count inconsistency between the new verdict bar chart and the existing scorecard/subtitle. The bar chart heading reads “How the 71 claimed wins actually land” while the subtitle says “Point-by-Point Analysis of 69 Claimed Wins” and the scorecard card says “69 Claimed Wins — The author claims 69 confirmed predictions and zero falsifications.” All three live on the Overview tab within one viewport of each other. Readers will notice. Cause: wins.json has 71 entries (IDs 001–069 plus 057b and 058b sub-entries), while the dome author’s headline WIN count on his current site is 69. The 2 sub-entries (057b = Global cavity re-pressurization, 058b = κ = 1.677 PRED-KAPPA-001) are real dome claims that collide with existing numbering on his site, so calling them ‘claimed wins’ is defensible — but the verdict-bar heading does not flag the split. A dome defender can cite this as “the reviewers silently inflated the denominator to 71 so they could tally more refutations.”
holes_found.recommendation: Either (a) change the bar chart heading to a phrase that unambiguously includes the two numbered-collision sub-claims (e.g. “How 71 catalog entries actually land — 69 top-line claims plus 2 numbered-collision sub-claims”, or a shorter “How the 71 catalogued claims actually land” with a footnote), or (b) add a small caption line under the bar chart explaining the 69→71 split, or (c) restrict the bar chart denominator to 69 by aggregating 057/057b and 058/058b. Option (a) with a footnote is cheapest. Whichever is chosen, every surface on the Overview tab (subtitle, scorecard, bar chart, Part 3 summary table) should use the same number and the same explanation.

holes_found.description: Rounded percentages on the verdict bar chart sum to 101% (31 + 25 + 20 + 13 + 6 + 6). Exact shares: Misleading 30.98, Std Model 25.35, Self-Contradicted 19.72, Refuted by Data 12.68, Not Demonstrated 5.63, Unfalsifiable 5.63. Each individual rounding is correct, but the visible total is off. Dome defenders who look for sloppy arithmetic will cite this as “the reviewers can’t even add percentages” — a cheap shot, but the shot is there to take.
holes_found.recommendation: Two options: (1) switch to one decimal place (31.0, 25.4, 19.7, 12.7, 5.6, 5.6 — total 100.0); or (2) keep whole numbers and add a small italic caption “(percentages rounded to whole numbers; total may differ from 100%)” below the bar chart. Option 2 is lower visual noise for a reviewer-document tone. Either way, once fixed, leave a comment in generateVerdictBarChart noting the chosen convention.

holes_found.description: The Unfalsifiable bar and the Misleading bar both href to #misleading because section 3.6 is combined (“Detailed: Misleading and Unfalsifiable”). The aria-label for Unfalsifiable says only “Unfalsifiable: 4 of 71 claims (6 percent). Click to view detailed analysis.” — no indication that the click will deliver the reader to a combined section. A screen-reader user clicking ‘Unfalsifiable’ and hearing the section heading ‘Misleading and Unfalsifiable’ will wonder whether they landed wrong. The generateVerdictBarChart code comment acknowledges this sharing explicitly, but that acknowledgement is not surfaced to users.
holes_found.recommendation: Update the aria-label for the Unfalsifiable and Misleading rows to mention the combined section. E.g. aria-label=“Unfalsifiable: 4 of 71 claims (6 percent). Click to view analysis in the combined Misleading and Unfalsifiable section.” Same pattern for the Misleading row. Cost: two strings in generateVerdictBarChart.

holes_found.description: Stance statement: “The world is a globe. The dome model fixes few or none of the fundamental problems of flat-earth cosmology. That does not prevent us from engaging with the specific claims it does make — and that is what this document does.” Two surface-level tensions. (1) ‘few or none’ is a hedge that sits uneasily with the scorecard’s ‘0 Actual Wins — Not one claim produces a result the globe model can’t explain and the dome uniquely can.’ A sharp reader notices ‘few or none’ immediately above a card asserting zero. Reconcilable — ‘fixes flat-earth problems’ is a different bar than ‘produces observations globe can’t explain’ — but the reader has to do that work. (2) ‘and that is what this document does’ is a slightly flat ending for a masthead sentence carrying as much editorial weight as this one. Not technically wrong on either count; the stance statement does not overclaim.
holes_found.recommendation: Optional. If kept as-is, the text is fine. If tightened: replace ‘few or none’ with ‘does not fix’ (matches the 0-wins card more tightly) and replace the final clause with something more active (‘— which is what this document does.’ already reads slightly better). Flag to the human group rather than patching; this is an editorial-voice judgement they explicitly own per EXP-208.

holes_found.description: EXP-210 palette neutralization: the --semantic-crit token (#7a2e2e light, #d46a6a dark) is a muted oxblood rather than the bright alarm-red of the pre-refresh #C62828 / #c45050 / #8b0000 palette. The bar-chart refuted bar and td.v-refuted cells now read as ‘restrained red’ rather than ‘fire engine red.’ Arguably the right call for document tone — and explicitly the stated goal of EXP-210 — but a dome defender reading the Overview could claim ‘the reviewers softened the severity signal on their refuted verdicts to look less hostile.’ In practice the red is still clearly red and the refuted category is carried by text + glyph under EXP-213’s color-plus-label rule, so severity is not actually reduced; but the perception hit exists for readers comparing old and new screenshots.
holes_found.recommendation: No change. Document the rationale in Section 3.2’s intro paragraph (one sentence noting that verdict-cell color is paired with a text label per WCAG, so the palette is restrained by design). This pre-empts the ‘you softened the hit’ attack.

holes_found.description: Std Model Explains bar uses green (--stdmodel-solid = #66BB6A light / #43a047 dark). In a general-audience reading, green reads as ‘good’ or ‘positive outcome.’ The legend clarifies — ‘mainstream physics already provides a complete, quantitative explanation… the dome model adds no predictive power’ — but a reader who sees a 25%-width green bar without reading the legend may form the impression that 25% of claims are ‘OK’ for the dome. In the review’s frame, Std Model Explains is a null-result verdict (no dome-specific contribution), not a positive outcome for the dome. The color does not lie, but it can miscue a casual reader.
holes_found.recommendation: Low priority. If addressed: either move the legend above the bar chart (so readers encounter the verdict definitions before the color-coded bars), or shift Std Model to a neutral blue-grey to avoid the ‘green = good’ heuristic. The current placement has legend below bars, below scorecard. Moving the legend up would require minor template reordering.

stronger_arguments.description: The Latest Findings demotion (EXP-212) is a good call editorially, but the operator could strengthen credibility by adding one line to the block explaining what it is: ‘We track the dome author’s reactive updates and our own new findings here.’ Currently the header is just ‘Latest Findings’ in small uppercase. A one-sentence caption under the header would recover the ‘moving target’ credibility signal without re-inflating the chrome.

The verdict bar chart’s sort-by-count order (Misleading 22 first, Refuted 9 fourth) is honest but visually buries the strongest verdict. A dome defender could weaponize this as ‘even the reviewers’ own chart doesn’t lead with refutation.’ Countermeasure: an optional ‘sort by severity’ toggle, or a one-line caption under the chart noting that ‘Refuted by Data is the most severe verdict; sorted here by count to show distribution shape.’ The caption is trivial and pre-empts the sort-order attack.

kernel_of_truth.description: The Phase A1 integration gets the editorial move right: a 69-claim critical review has no business feeling like a real-time operations dashboard. The stance-statement reframes the document as argument rather than telemetry. The bar chart replaces six uniform scorecard cards with a comparison shape that actually uses the 22:4 ratio. The EXP-210 palette restriction correctly confines red to semantic crit surfaces. The Latest Findings demotion stops the page from shouting BREAKING NEWS before the reader reads an argumentative sentence. Individually these are textbook moves; collectively they pull the document out of the ‘SaaS admin panel that happens to critique cosmology’ register it had been drifting into.
kernel_of_truth.why_it_doesnt_save_claim: Getting editorial tone right is necessary but not load-bearing for the review’s factual content. The design refresh could be perfect and the underlying claims could still be wrong; conversely the design could be ugly and the review could still be right. The scientific content is where dome defenders will attack. The design refresh earns its keep only if it (a) stops giving defenders free hits on tone (‘your dashboard UI betrays your bias’ argument, which Phase A1 partly neutralizes) and (b) does not introduce new numeric or logical inconsistencies — the 71-vs-69 claim-count mismatch in Finding 1 above is exactly that kind of self-inflicted wound.

text_fingerprint.bar_chart_heading: How the 71 claimed wins actually land
text_fingerprint.latest_findings_position: below scorecard and verdict-legend (line 389 in docs/index.html)

deeper_analysis: Adversarial read-through of each Phase A1 acceptance criterion against the rendered HTML. (1) EXP-202 tokens — --serif, --sans, --mono, --ink*, --rule, --semantic-* all present in :root and dark media block, WOFF2 @font-face declarations present (Source Serif 4 variable, Inter variable, JetBrains Mono variable). No dangling old literals. OK. (2) EXP-207 role assignment — body uses var(--serif), UI chrome classes (.tab-btn, .section-nav a, etc.) use var(--sans), .formula uses var(--mono). sections.json prose blobs no longer carry font-family:monospace literals; .formula class is applied where monospace was previously inline. OK. (3) EXP-208 stance-statement — present at line 289 inside title-block, above the H1 ‘Critical Review’; CSS at line 78 styles it as italic serif, max-width 56ch, ink-2 color. Text reads as editorial preamble, not as shouting. Mobile media query at line 205 scales it down appropriately. OK — minor editorial-voice note in Finding 4 above. (4) EXP-209 bar chart — generateVerdictBarChart exists, {{VERDICT_BAR_CHART}} placeholder is substituted, rendered chart is present at line 298 with heading, caption, and six aria-labeled rows. Counts check live: 71 total, tally sums to 71, each width% is count/max (so the 22-count Misleading bar is 100% width, correct). Sort is count-desc with alphabetical tiebreak per the comparator. Two issues flagged in Findings 1–3 above (71-vs-69 mismatch, 101% rounding, shared-anchor aria-label). (5) EXP-210 red-chrome neutralization — no #c45050 / #8b0000 remain in docs/index.html or sections.json. --refuted maps to --semantic-crit-soft, --refuted-solid to --semantic-crit. tag-monitoring-hardcoded migrated to --semantic-warn (amber) per spec. Kill-shot ‘Refuted by Data’ inline badges route through --semantic-crit. Border-left accents on stat cards now use var(--rule). OK — minor perception concern in Finding 5. (6) EXP-212 Latest Findings demotion — .breaking-news border is 1px var(--rule), background var(--card-bg), no gradient. .bn-header is .78rem uppercase ink-2 color, no emoji. Block position is below scorecard and verdict-legend, above the TOC. ‘Moving target’ signal preserved via three dated entries. OK — strengthen-argument note above about adding a one-line caption. (7) EXP-213 accessibility — CSS includes :focus-visible, .vt-glyph / .vb-glyph, .skip-link, .stacked-card-table @media block at 720px, tap-target rules on .toc a / .tab-btn / table-cell .win-anchor. Verdict cells now carry glyph-plus-label rather than color-alone. Could not verify every contrast ratio by eye, but the token choices (#7a2e2e on #f2dada light, #d46a6a on #3d1f1f dark) read as WCAG AA compliant under quick visual check — a proper contrast audit is the integrity agent’s beat, not the curmudgeon’s. OK with deference to the integrity agent for formal audit. Overall verdict: Phase A1 integration is solid; the one material issue is the 71-vs-69 claim-count inconsistency introduced by the new bar chart, which must be reconciled before the design refresh can be called finished.

citations_unchecked: EXP-213 accessibility WCAG AA contrast ratios — formal audit deferred to the integrity agent per its prompt-file scope

review_notes: target_type in queue was ‘exp-integrated’, not one of the documented target_types in curmudgeon.md (win-new/win-detail-rewrite/section-new/section-rewrite/proposal/killshot-*/prediction-batch/research). Treated as a proposal-package-style spot-check across the integrated bundle per the reason-string instructions from the decider. Trigger set to ‘priority-queue-expansion-prose’ as the closest documented known-trigger; queue_id=233 included per PROP-009.
